chunks.text: All right, well, we'll usually see then. No, you know. Good to go. Yeah. Are you dispensing goodwill to the committee? No. Reva was trying to buy goodwill. What are you going to do to her? No, I don't need it. Not yet. Right. Well, first of all, welcome, Minister.

We've got you until 2:30, so we've got 30 minutes with you. You know the drill, of course. Hopefully you won't speak for too long, and then we'll get into questions. I don't really intend to speak at all. I suppose just to introduce Anna Adams KC, who's the new Solicitor General. Started when?

Oh, six weeks ago. Six weeks ago. Congratulations. I started eight weeks ago, roughly. I forget the exact date. And I've got Robert Glennie, who's the acting DCE for services and stuff here, and I've got the whole team from Crown Law behind me, and we've got PCO here as well, because I think we're doing PCO straight after this at half past two.

So I think everyone knows what Crown Law does. It's a real privilege to hold the Attorney role. And I'm a bit new into it, so is Anna, but she obviously comes from long experience in the law and we've inherited an institution that is obviously very important to the rule of law, and the conduct of Crown, and I think is doing a good job.

So I'm really in your hands. Right. Well, I'm going to start off with Ms. Vanusi Walters. Thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you, Minister, and congratulations both to the Minister on his new appointment and to funding Solicitor General as well. And just to open up, I also think it's worthwhile acknowledging the landmark settlement in terms of the Stafford case, now that we're going to discuss as well today.

Firstly, I'd say that it's positive to see the slight uplift in the budget, so the 3.9%, and of course the headline questions are, is it enough? And is it being directed to the places where we're really seeing we need that capacity? And maybe the context of that question relates to the response to question 14, where there's an identification that there's a need for, or there's risks around Crown prosecution and the Criminal Appeals Service, but no additional funding in that particular part of the budget line.

I'm just wondering if you can speak to that. But also just generally that larger question as well. Just start with the second one. What's the larger question? You just more generally is there enough money? The larger question is, is it enough- Yeah... and is it being directed to the right places? But that's the particular example in this case. Yeah. I think you're aware of the government's fiscal objectives.

And I think Crown Law is doing a good job managing within tight constraints like every government agency. Who's best placed to talk for the 14, which is the sustainability of Crown prosecution and criminal appeal? Anna, or do you want to speak to that?

Criminal deputy solicitor general. Do you want to come up to the front here? Get their names. Yeah. Sorry, can you just say your full name for the record? Yes. Madeleine Laracy, Deputy Solicitor General Criminal. Thank you. It's the bulk of the vote. Yeah. 71% of Crown Law's money goes out to Crown solicitors.

Yeah. So the question relates to, uh- The question relates to the fact that it's an identified risk that there might not be sufficient capacity for Crown prosecutions and criminal appeal services. Yes. But in the breakdowns, it doesn't look to me like those appeals have had an increase in funding. So the increase has gone to the constitutional and the other team, essentially.

That's right. We had an increase to the bulk fund, which funds Crown prosecutions, which are conducted around the country by Crown solicitors about three or four years ago. That is a capped fund, and it hasn't increased since then. Undoubtedly, there is a steady percentage increase in the volume of cases Crown solicitors prosecute, which is the more serious ones, and we manage the sustainability of that service by monitoring that workload and pretty carefully.

So we don't see any occasion to be suggesting that it's got to the point of being unsustainable at the moment. But we do have forecasting that suggests in a year or so we will have to be looking at that carefully again if volumes continue to increase. For the appeals work, much like the criminal trials that they come from, criminal appeals are increasing in volume year on year and in complexity. One of the mechanisms we have for managing within the money available in the criminal appeals appropriation, which is separate, is we can

either do the appeals ourself, which we do most of, and certainly the more complex ones, or we brief them mainly to Crown solicitors, and we have very cost-effective arrangements for doing that. Essentially, we will put a cap on how many hours they can charge us for, regardless of how much time they spent. There is a judgment there in particular cases, but there are arrangements that are extremely beneficial for the Crown to deal with criminal appeals.

Did you have a follow-up? Thank you. So with the Public Prosecution Service, the funding stayed the same, and I understand that maintaining cost. In the last cycle, there was a requirement to make some savings. Which team was that- Uh, which team was that made to, just to get a benchmark?

So we went through about four organizational restructures in the last two years, focused on enabling services. So, we define frontline services as legal counsel, Crown Law. So this was corporate services really taking the brunt of the savings. Yeah.

So not to public prosecution services. No. Okay. Thank you. Right. You all right with that? Yeah. Right. I'm going to- Fewer executive assistants, basically. Is that right? Fewer- Fewer-... support services, yes... corporate services. Okay. Yeah. Okay. I'm going to Joseph Maney. Yeah, thank you. And, look, it's just a follow-on question, noting that it's approximately 71% of Crown funding is distributed to the Crown Solicitors Network.

And look, just from the context of my own background, I was on the Crown Prosecution panel, but primarily did defense law for about a decade. Something that would really de-complexify things would be the evidence provided early and comprehensively. Because there's a real game, frankly, that goes on with not providing disclosure early, and the Criminal Procedure Act doesn't actually require it for a plea, and that's a issue in itself. But repeatedly, and I did a lot of jury trials, repeatedly, evidence would only be provided at the

last minute or, in fact, during the jury trial. That leaves a lot of complexity. So could I just ask if there's any thinking around addressing that fundamental underlying issue? It's more of a policy or operational issue rather than a funding, but- Well, it comes back to the funding, because some of the funding of complexities because of this underlying issue.

You're probably right. I definitely rec- of course, we all, anyone in the criminal justice system, whatever side they're on in terms of being a party or a judge, recognizes the problem you're mentioning. The Crown Prosecution Network certainly uses the levers it has. It's important to remember, obviously, in this context, that the body that has the material that's available for disclosure is not the prosecutors.

That's the investigative material. Mm-hmm. And that is held by police. Mm-hmm. Police are working to develop various processes and have developed what we call the, what, CJSU, Criminal Justice- Support Unit... Support Unit, yes, thank you. Which is responsible for disclosure and great bulk of certainly the less serious cases, and they have various other mechanisms to support the Chief Justice's practice note, to make sure that disclosure is happening.

That, and possibly more attention now in terms of the recognition that difficult disclosure cases, which literally just flow from the fact, as you'll be well aware, that so many more cases involve great volumes of electronic evidence. Mm. Those cases need careful judicial management as well, and there are increasing processes to ensure that.

But of course, that just adds to judge time. But Crown Prosecutors, where they are aware that disclosure needs to happen and hasn't happened, are under very solemn expectations, which they fully understand, to make sure that police do disclose material. What is disclosed late, in my experience, is often reports that have come in late or formal written statements that weren't available earlier, or material that hadn't been recognized as being relevant until issues crystallized. But certainly accept that the Crown has a

important part to play here. If I could ask one little supplementary on that. Okay. Chair, just very briefly. And thank you for that. That's a very good articulation, I think, of some of the issues. But, and recognizing it's usually the instructing agency- Mm... that is responsible for the disclosure. But is there a piece of work being considered to address that fundamental issue and working with those agencies to develop a better practice?

There is actually. About three or four years ago, we set up with police, for instance, a senior Crown solicitors working with some senior people in police, a disclosure working group to not only help me finalize some guidance, which we put out to all Crown Prosecutors, but also to be identifying the types of cases where disclosure issues are routinely arising or trying to sort of anticipate what the future disclosure issues are going to be.

So that's one mechanism we have. The other mechanism, of course, is that disclosure issues are being increasingly litigated. So our office, me in particular, pay a lot of attention to that case law, and then thinking about what are all prosecutors' responsibilities, not just Crown Prosecutors, based on the case law that we see evolving.

Thank you. Okay. Now I'm going back to Vanushi Walters. Thank you. Just recognizing that in terms of predicting for future workflow, this is one of the areas where it's very difficult because you're responding essentially. However, there is one area where the work is either generated or not, depending on what the government passes in terms of legislation and what kind of litigation risk it presents. Are we seeing an increasing number of judicial reviews taken against the government in recent times, and is that why we're seeing an uplift in the

budget relating to constitutional issues? For the first question. That's a good question. Who wants to deal with that? Anna? I can. Tara? Yeah, I can address the funding at least. So the funding is really due to a technical transfer, an expense transfer. So we've taken some surplus from last year and moved it across to next year and then two route years to help fund a new legal practice management system.

So that is really the driver of that movement and funding. Otherwise, baseline is static really. No savings required, but also no new initiatives. Yeah. And the question about an increasing number of JR's. And I'll pass that on to- Do we actually have data on that? I don't know. Do we, Aaron?

Not specifically, but there is an uptick in the- There is an uptick. Plus the tribunal. I'm told there's an uptick. It's probably true. You'd be aware that there is a significant volume of quite significant litigation for the courts at the moment. We are waiting on four decisions around public law compensation and criminal cases, Patua, Fitzgerald, Rapanui, and Porori.

It'd be fair to say we are dealing with a significant range of quite complicated public law and human rights issues, some of which have arisen through litigation, others not. So- Well... there's not a lot the Crown can do about that. I get sued. I'm the most sued man in New Zealand. Perhaps just as a supplementary, Minister, there is another way that the Crown can potentially manage its litigation costs, and that's by legislating to prevent cases proceeding up to more senior courts.

Now, there's government view on that, but of course, the attorney has a special role to play in our democracy, and I wonder if you'd comment on whether you believe that that's a constitutionally appropriate way in which for the government to manage its- You're talking about Smith? I am. Yeah, I'm very comfortable with it. All right. Now I'm going to go to Dr. Sun.

Thank you. Thank you, Chair. Just following up from what the previous speakers asked, and your response around the static, I guess, nature of the Law Office constitutional criminal law duties. In the supplementary estimates, there is the estimates and the supplementary estimates see a reduction of, or savings of 2.7 million.

Which again, is what you're saying has now been supplemented. But considering that there was an underspend in the last financial year, why does it need to be increased from what was 12.4, I think, now to 13.7? Yeah, we do run a full multi-category appropriation.

Yeah. So we can shift between categories there. But yeah, the funding is there to implement a new legal practice management system and the cloud licensing associated with that. Just a few more in terms of the budget side. One of the things we do see there has been a reduction of is in the public prosecution service for the provision and supervision of national Crown Prosecution Service and oversight of public prosecution, particularly a reduction for improving the justice response to sexual violence victims.

Why is there been a reduction in that? Carry on. That's an initiative back to Budget '19. So that was a structured diminishing initiative. So this is the last year of reductions. It was front-loaded to allow for training and getting it set up, and then a reducing sort of portfolio over the years. So this is the last year of the reductions. Yeah.

Is that from a perspective that people are now trained and that it's no longer... Or is that no longer necessary when it comes to justice response to sexual violence victims? There's still ongoing funding. It's just hit now the last level of reduction, and now it'll be static.

So there's still allowed for the volume of cases to come through and to address sexual violence, family violence victims. Final question just around this. I think this is one of the key areas we've heard from the long-term insight briefing, and now coming to now is the increase in terms of complexity of cases.

But also, one of the things we heard previously is this increase in the number of people who are potentially using AI, self-representing, and all of those. Is this something that Crown Law has factored into how they're going to be able to litigate and how they're able to... The kind of capacity or burden issue it plays on Crown Law, and has that been factored into this year's budget?

I don't think it's been factored into the budget, but it's a... I don't know. Do you want to comment on AI? Anyone or someone? Erin? In terms of Crown Prosecution- Oh, sorry. Specifically. Yeah. These issues mainly arise in the context of more pretrial applications.

Yeah. So Crown solicitors are dealing with them. They're arguing those. We measure the total time that the Crown Prosecution Network is spending on cases over the course of the year. As we see the total hours increasing and the total number of cases increasing, that does go into, obviously, our overall sort of assessment of at what point does the current funding become unsustainable.

Okay. But we're not sort of in a position to single out the impact on the Crown Prosecution Service of challenges that have been raised, say, by AI in the context of evidence. Okay. We're looking at total- But what about from your perspective, how much are you using AI?

Are you using that to help mitigate some of the cost escalation you're seeing because of the complexity? Has it been embraced within the Department? I could speak to that. In the legal environment, AI is both a complicating factor and a solution.

So in legal research, for example, our library is trialing the same AI system as the courts are, which essentially it's a legally research function orientated around case law, and it makes researching incredibly quick- Yeah... for then absorption by librarians and lawyers, and it speeds the process up.

In the document and evidential analysis space, AI is also quite a revolutionary solution to very large volumes of evidence. For example, these days you have a legal AI program called Harvey, which can essentially take all of the documents loaded into a database for a particular case, put them in a document vault, and then enable the lawyers on the case to query the vault- Yeah... instead of reading the documents.

Cool And then cross-check the answers it gives them against it. So, the Crown Law at the moment is in a process of trialing these different solutions. They will make a difference to legal practice generally, and obviously as an organization, we have to keep up with it. So I see them more as solutions rather than problems, but there is, of course, many ethical and legal concerns around actually using AI directly without human involvement in legal document.

To be careful, I think it was a Supreme Court case the other day in which a self-represented litigant cited a bunch of cases that were generated by AI that were made up. Phantom. Mm-hmm. Yeah. Phantom cases. Was it Supreme Court? But the reality is- Senior Court of Appeal One, we- Senior Court of Appeal... we spent hours- Yeah... trying to work out- Find it... these citations were true or false.

Yeah. Yeah. But that doesn't- Yeah... good AI practice will always have a role of a human. That's the number one component in it. But in terms of the people you're opposing in court, they'll be using it as much as anyone. So it should be regarded, I presume, as a way of at least dealing with a lot of the research, as you quite rightly say, but also framing up arguments.

But ultimately, you all have your prosecutors who all have deeper knowledge of what's going on, I presume. But anyone who's not using AI must be well behind the ball park. It's an essential part of legal practice increasingly. And the courts themselves issued a protocol about it, I think it was last year, giving guidance to when lawyers can use AI and- Yeah... when they must cite that they did use it.

And so I think we do have a framework in New Zealand for the use of AI in litigation. But yes, if Crown Law is practicing in this space as it must do and it must lead in this space, then it needs to absorb AI like every other firm. Thank you. We're going to Todd Stevenson.

Oh, thank you. Oh, if you're okay, Anna, I'm going to ask you some questions. Sure. Sorry, Minister. But I want to say thanks for taking on this role, and actually thank you for your service on the Farming Board. But actually staying a little bit with this theme, you've come obviously in from private practice. Workforce capability within Crown Law, what have you observed?

Are there any kind of skill gaps or things that you're thinking about that are going to need to change in the next year or two around the types of people you have employed or the skill sets you need, particularly with some of the changes, say, in technology? Not immediately, and my impression is that Crown Law is a wellspring of legal talent and expertise.

They're extremely talented lawyers spread across the Auckland and Wellington offices. Obviously, our practice has to evolve depending on the demand that comes in from the government. In the last couple of years, I can see, looking at the patterns, that there is an extremely high demand in the area of infrastructure, natural resources, planning, treaty cases, public law cases.

So that naturally means that we have to shape our legal workforce to meet that demand. But that's something we have in common with all legal practices. And of course, we run a national practice across both offices, and we have, I guess, what you might call a porous membrane with the wider legal profession in that people work at Crown Law, and then they work elsewhere in practice, and I think that's a good thing. It strengthens and diversifies the organization.

Which is actually just one more supplementary, which is actually going to be about retention of talent, because yeah, obviously they may come to Crown Law, get a great foundation, then move into private practice. Have you, yeah, thought about ways of either attracting people back or trying to retain them with different initiatives? I think the thing that retains people at Crown Law is the quality and the interest of the work.

Mm-hmm. I think that really is what does it. Yeah. But, I don't see it as a bad thing that people might come and go. I think that actually diversifies their experience and strengthens their different variety of legal perspectives. And particularly with litigation, it's actually a real skill builder for any lawyer to practice in different environments and subject areas. So...

Historically, Crown Law has been regarded as a great, training ground is the wrong phrase, but a great place to work because the quality of the work you work on- Yeah... sort of almost by definition is interesting and intellectually stimulating. And that's a good thing.

And as Anna says, in my view, it's not a bad thing that people do some time at Crown Law and go off into either corporate world or in-house counsel at another government agency or on their own account or whatever, and then come and go. But that's good.

Then, it probably helps re-energize Crown Law as people come and go a bit. So, that's a positive thing. All right. I started as a summer clerk at Crown Law. Did you? I did. Oh, gosh. Back when they took summer clerks. Yeah. Make way up for a full-time job. Maybe we should bring it back. We've got five minutes left, so I've got two questions from Vanusi Walters.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. And I didn't get to ask this supplementary earlier, but I wonder, Minister, you'd expand on your answer to my last question. About Smith? About Smith. Sure. Because it sounded to me like the minister is saying that there is no constitutional difference if the government legislate before a case reaches the Supreme Court versus after. Now, in my view, those are quite different things.

Can you frame this in terms of the budget? That the- Or maybe- This is money or- No. It's a forward-looking cost- Yeah, okay... in terms of managing litigation, and it felt to me that the minister was saying that this is an appropriate way in which to manage future litigation risk, which attaches to future litigation costs.

Okay. Well, look, it's actually a relatively straightforward issue. We have the Supreme Court judgment, which has left open the idea that, a tort of climate damage could be developed. The government's view, I think reasonably, is that we have an entire statutory schema to deal with climate change, the Climate Change Response Act and the Emissions Trading Scheme, and that for very good public policy reasons, allowing the courts to develop in an ad hoc way, potentially develop, and I stress potentially, because the Supreme Court didn't actually rule on that,

they just said it's open. That the prospect of that is destabilizing for business confidence and for the proper development of the law. And so you kind of got two options at that point. You can either say, "Okay. Well, that's interesting. We can let the court develop or let the law develop and come back in eight to 10 years, and potentially legislate over the top of it."

In the meantime, a degree of chaos ensues. Or you can just cut to the quick and deal with the issue now, and Parliament can act to make sure that that doesn't happen through putting in place a statutory bar and provide legal certainty, and that's the decision that the government has taken. And that, to me, feels like an appropriate course of action.

There's no fruits of litigation to get rid of. Well, so the minister doesn't believe that it would've been the more constitutionally appropriate thing to let the court case proceed to the Supreme Court, which it's scheduled to do next year? No, I think part of the problem, in the development of the law over the last few years in New Zealand, is that Parliament has been too unwilling to legislate when they think the law has taken a wrong course.

Ultimately in our constitution, the elected chamber, the elected legislature is sovereign, and Parliament is supreme, and if Parliament thinks that the law is taking an incorrect course, then they should say so, and that's what legislation ultimately is. Okay, so something new?

Something new. All right. So this is a question about the Financial Sustainability programs, the FSP, which was initially put in place only in July 2024. That shifted then to the Organizational Change Improvement team, which has, I understand, a broader mandate. Does that mandate include, the scope of looking at restructure and redundancy?

What does the broadening of that mandate? Sorry. No, you want a go. It does have a broader mandate than the Financial Sustainability Program, yes. I guess the foundation of it is a similar concept, but we did establish the Financial Sustainability Program to look at surviving within a reduced baseline.

So that was really the nexus of that. So we went through some organizational change. We've looked at process improvements to help make more efficient legal services. And then this has sort of spurned the need for a Organizational Change and Improvement team, and that one of the biggest projects is this legal practice management system that they will look after.

If there is a need, then potentially this team could look after or help support some organizational restructuring if required. Certainly, but that's not the purpose of it, no. Okay. Right. We're at 2:30. I'm going to thank the officials. Thank you.

New Solicitor General. Thank you for coming in. Now, you're obviously going to stay there, Minister. I'll stay. Let's swap over. We've got PCO. We've got some more lawyers. We'll do that transition. Thank you. It's good to see you. We will see you again. Watch as well if you want.

Didn't mind. Pass on the bat up, too. Yep. Now we've got PCO here. We love PCO. We do. We do. They do the hard yards. Very exciting. We're doing a statutory spring clean. I loved that release. Hope you all saw my press release. I did. I read it. I'm a guy who likes smaller government, so we're making a vague start on it.

150 outdated or obsolete laws are going. You must read my press release. It's full of very fun puns. Including about the Wellington Milk Supply Act 2019, but there's a whole list here. Duncan, you'll be very upset, the Canterbury Society of Arts Preserve Act 1889, Extension Act 1891. That's on the chopping block, my friend That's now towards the center of contemporary arts, actually, but- Likewise, the Christchurch City Reclamation and Empowering Act 1964, that's gone Adding 1964 It's going to go. Sumner Borough Land Vesting Act.

I haven't seen any in a while- Did you throw in Rebecca Carter in there as well? The Dunedin City Fish Markets and Empowering Act 1918. As if there wasn't enough on World War I at the time, Parliament passed a law to allow the Dunedin City Council to engage in the fish trade, and also buy and sell sheep. So anyway, we've been running this- Oh, no... I'm taking the p**s a little bit, but also it is quite funny, and quite useful.

PCO have been running this repeals project since, when did you start it? 2020? About 12 months. 12 months ago. Have you got more to go? We've got 152 have been identified. We're consulting on them now, and then you've got another 50 coming. Mm-hmm. And there'll be more after that. There'll be more after that. There will be.

Yeah. It gets harder and harder as we get into the acts. Often there's land, so you have to work out whether that land still exists, who holds that land now, and what's happened to it. Mm-hmm. So there's a lot of work to do. It is quite important. Our statute books has grown like Topsy.

Mm-hmm. Yeah, there's too many laws. Too many words. You can get a few from earlier in this term. What's that? I would welcome that. Oh, well, they're not on the chopping block. But the Wellington milk supply thing is quite funny, because, a lawyer actually applied for a license, and there was a lot-- that's actually quite a genuine issue, because he said, "Well, the act says you've got to issue me a license."

And, the city council said, "Well, you've got to be joking." And he said, "Well, the law's the law. I want a license to sell milk." I think it's an exclusive license. Is that right? I don't know, actually. There was only one act. So, it would have been exclusive. So anyway, we had to do a deal in which he-- well, he took action in the courts, and we had to reach an agreement with them to seek to remove the law, which we're now doing. But, every now and then you think ooh- So that's good stuff, and no doubt members have got lots of questions on that.

Is there anything else you want to highlight for the committee this morning? No, I just wanted to highlight that. All right. Good. Right. We're going to start up with Mr. Tom Rutherford, and then we'll go to over here to my good friend. Yeah. Thank you, Mr. Chair. I'd be interested to know all 152 of them. It's on the back of my press release. You can see it. Taking that- I'm very excited about it... I'm providing the minister the opportunity to- No, no.

No, no. I think we've got better uses of time. No other questions, Mr. Rutherford? I'll come back. I've got a supplementary. Really? It's a serious supplementary. A serious one, okay. Mr. Chair. No, mine is a serious supplementary. Thank you for your work on this. But obviously going forward into the year that we're now talking about, the estimates- Yep. What happened... yeah, how much of your time will be spent on this, and obviously have you set aside money in the budget to continue to do this?

Well, we have at least one and a half people on this work at the moment. So, yeah. I had two fake questions from government members. Well, your interpretation of fake is different to mine. You've got 25 minutes. Have you finished your question? We've got a time limit. Yes, they're answering. Please, quiet everyone. Yeah, please, let's finish. We've got one and a half people? Yes. And there is some research work to do- Yeah... to get to the end of it. But it's valuable work, and what will happen at the end is that we will publish results of that, even if it doesn't

result in the repeal, so people know why it stayed on the statute book. Yeah. So it's important legal research and interpretation. I'm glad you said that. Thank you. Right. Now I'm going to Dr. Sun Nun, please. Thank you. Thank you, Chair. I think a lot of this comes from the new funding and the drafting and access to legislation initiative, and I want to check, and I know this is something that Camilla, you had questions on as well in terms of amendments.

Has there been an increase in terms of workload for the PCO, and there's also the fact in this budget when it comes to the drafting of legislation. Yes. But also in terms of what would that mean in the interaction with, for example, the Regulatory Standards Act, and how has that been put both in terms of capacity, but also urgency and time-wise burden for the PCO?

The short answer is, I'll deal with the first one, is yes, there is. And, 2025, there was an out-of-cycle funding increase for immediate cost pressures for legislative delivery, 1.292 million. So 10 FTE on the back of that have been onboarded, with a further drafting recruitment round underway as well.

So the short answer is yes, Parliament is passing more legislation and, as I think you know, Lawrence, passing it more quickly, all of which requires drafting resource. And, what was the second question? It was quite a good one. I think the second one is whether then this runs into both capacity, but also in terms of the kind of urgency we're seeing in terms of a lot of the legislation, whether that also adds additional pressure in terms of the 10 FTEs that you just mentioned, Minister, and how would that work in terms of the spread

of those 10 FTEs? Well, you do things more... I mean, just to make a general point, and Cassie might want to comment. But if you do things more quickly, you've got to get them right by definition- Mm-hmm... and that requires resource to check. And by definition, if you do things more quickly, sometimes you've got to go and fix them, all of which requires drafting resource.

I'll just make that as a general point. Cassie might want to comment on it. No, absolutely, Attorney. I mean, I absolutely second to what the Attorney said, but also, I suppose, simply adding more FTE probably isn't our long-term solution. Like, it's an important aspect, and it's been really significant to be able to do that drafting recruitment round, and another one this year.

But we are looking at digital tools as well to try and get it to a more sustainable approach, ensuring that we can automate some of our low-hanging fruit quality review tool, explanatory note- Mm... first draft generator. These are important aids, I guess, to helping to make sure that the drafting FTE is focused on the hard work and the low- This is AI, right?

AI, yeah. I would like to know more about that, but I guess the Does PCO see that as being a concern, and a concern addressed with the Attorney General on this potentially every successive government, every term, not even successive, every term, the new government will want to play a top trump and in creating even more legislation, and we're going to be seeing more and more and more. Is that something that PCO has raised concerns with or is aware of?

Well, we've certainly made it sure that the attorney and others in the government's generally aware of the quantity of legislation- Yeah... coming through, and we've done some research on that. And that's transparently sort of obvious, I think, to the public, and I think it's a broader system question, really, about what the House can actually- To get you done. We'll go to- Oh, okay.

Go to- Yep, sure. We'll give you, I'll give you a number, sorry, that you'll be interested in this. In 2001, there were 15,000 words on the statute books. 15 million. Sorry, 15 mil- Million. Sorry, 15 million. That was a, yep. That would be nice. A bit more than a million. No, no, no. Yep, 15 million.

And by 2024 or '5, we'd have got up to 23 million. Yeah. So- The new ones- It's quite a jump... or words you'd found? Eh? Were they new words or words you'd found? Well, the Repeals Project will help delete some of that. Yeah. Good on you. Don't know how many. Probably not that much.

Let's go to the new- It's relatively short lists. So the scale of legislation is one thing, and the numbers are increasing. The other issue is the way in which bills are managed through the House, and- Okay, this is a budget question. It is. It relates to capacity. Budget pace of the bills going up. It relates to capacity. Yeah, so looking forward to the new fiscal year, there are four ministerial priorities that remain clear and at the top of my mind.

These are to increase prison capacity, ensure staffing levels remain proportional to the prison population, improve staff safety, and reduce re-offending through effective rehabilitation. Mm. The government is committed to community safety and restoring law and order. We are unapologetic about making sure that those who need to be in prison are in prison, and I'm resolute in ensuring Corrections has the resources to manage the increasing prison population.

Since November 2023, Corrections has added 2,837 beds, with another 240 beds expected by the end of this year. This builds on the government's support for the accelerated capacity program at Hawkes Bay Regional Prison and on the commitment to invest in the expansion of capacity at Waikeria Prison and the Christchurch Men's Prison.

We've also had the highest number of corrections officers on the front line. That is 4,774 in service at 31 May 2026, with the government investing directly into the front line through subsequent budgets as well. This includes an additional 710 staff in this year's budget, with 471 of these being corrections officers.

We have noted an increase in the number of prisoners associated with trans-national organized crime Whose approach continues to be attempting to get what they want through influence and bribery. We have seen recently how persistent and unnerving these prisoners are. Corrections has placed significant focus on integrity and ensuring that staff have the tools and knowledge to identify and then report when they are approached by prisoners or members of the community attempting to manipulate them.

I wholeheartedly support this work and know that Rachel and her team have a plan to respond and strengthen Corrections' training and oversight of this key area. Staff safety has also been a fundamental priority. Corrections staff deserve to return home safely at the end of every shift, and we want to do everything we can to make sure that happens.

That is why I led the Corrections Amendment Bill through the House last year, which included introducing new intelligence provisions, expanding body imaging searches to reduce contraband, and improving disciplinary processes. In addition, I'm now leading the changes through the Corrections, Management of Prisoners and Prisoners Property Amendment Bill, which will ensure Corrections has a robust legislative framework to manage the small number of prisoners who pose an extreme risk to prison and public safety.

Together, these changes will help support a more secure prison system and give frontline staff the tools and protections they need to improve their safety. Finally, while imprisonment can be an important means to restoring law and order and keeping the community safe, effective rehabilitation also plays a critical part in reducing re-offending and preparing people for successful reintegration into society upon release.

That is why the government has passed legislation to ensure that remand convicted prisoners are provided with rehabilitation that addresses the drivers of their offending. I want to finish by acknowledging the professionalism, resilience, and dedication of Corrections staff across the country.
